dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for stroke/cva.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My dad said things were going a little funny with his words. He looked confused at lunchtime.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and the day of the week?
His name's Robert. He wasn't really himself. Just stared and mumbled something odd.
Alright. Mr. Robert, what country are we in?
Pupils are reactive and equal bilaterally.
Mr. Robert, what is the date today?
Uh, he's blinking and breathing; not sure what he said. But he sounds funny.
Can you squeeze my finger, Mr. Robert?
He squeezed my finger pretty well on both hands. I think he's trying to respond.
Ma'am, can I look into his mouth for a second to make sure his airway is clear?
Airway is clear and patent. He can make sounds if asked.
You're going to feel the scope on your chest a bit, Mr. Robert. Just relax and breathe normally.
Respiration rate is 14, breath sounds are clear bilaterally.
Trachea is midline. No signs of respiratory distress.
Blood pressure is 170 over 108.
Heart rate is 66, strong pulse. No signs of poor perfusion.
Pulse noted at 66 with regular rhythm.
How long ago did the problem with your speech start, Mr. Robert?
Just about when we sat down for lunch. He sounded like he was having trouble organizing words.
Did he feel any pain or discomfort at the time?
He's saying he feels absolutely fine. Just shaky with his words.
What is his past medical history, ma'am?
He has high blood pressure and diabetes. Takes insulin.
Any medications right now, ma'am?
Yeah, last dose of Lantus and metformin early this morning.
Has he had anything to eat or drink in the last hour or so?
He had coffee about 15 minutes ago, but that was it.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad is your pain right now, Mr. Robert?
Mr. Robert, are you feeling any pain in your head or limbs?
He said he feels zero pain and is ready to stand if we ask.
Let me just shine this light in your eyes real quick for a better look.
Eyes are PERRL. Trachea is midline.
He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Glasgow Coma Scale result is 15 (E4, V5, M6).
All limbs are moving well. No deformities or signs of trauma.
Respirations remain at 14 per minute.
Blood pressure is still at 170 over 110.
Rhythm is sinus.
Oxygen saturation is 96% on room air.
Blood sugar is 163.
I'll place an IV lock on his arm, is that okay, Mr. Robert?
IV lock successfully placed. Using normal saline.
We are managing per stroke/TIA protocol based on field signs.
Still 14 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 170/110 again.
Sinus rhythm remains on the 4-lead heart monitor.
We're going to the Stroke Centre at St. Mary's Hospital in Richmond.
We have him secured to the stretcher in a semi-Fowler's position.
Mr. Robert is ready for transport. His vitals and medications are on record.
Hospital was contacted earlier. RN knows we're on our way.
Based on physical examination, a field diagnosis of acute infarction is being considered.
He has dysphasia noted on initial evaluation.
His oxygen saturation remains stable on assessment.
Patient has no signs or symptoms of limb movement impairment.
Mr. Robert, we'll reassess your condition in regular intervals throughout transport.
No new symptoms were reported in the last check in.
His medical history is unchanged. No new medications or allergies.
IV lock remains patent, no further interventions necessary.
Patient remains in a stable position ready for CT scan once at the hospital.
We're going to require a CT scan for confirmation.
Patient safety and comfort is maintained throughout transport to ensure a smooth CT scan process.
Upon arrival, he will be moved directly to imaging for evaluation of potential infarction.
Patient will require ongoing follow-up post-CT scan.
We are monitoring his mental status during transport.
Per EMS protocol, we have documented all necessary steps and prepared for post-arrival management.
The GCS score is consistent and likely to remain so during transport.
We are planning for a CT scan as part of the stroke protocol upon hospital arrival.
There are no contraindications for CT scan at this time.
Patient is being transported in line with stroke management expectations.
All pre-hospital care steps are consistent with Stroke/TIA protocol.
The field impression is based on signs including dysphasia, GCS 15, and stroke-like symptoms.
We recommend immediate neuroimaging as the suspected condition is stroke.
Expected once at the hospital, a CT scan will be conducted as agreed between us and the receiving hospital.
The protocol includes transport to a facility with acute stroke capability.
No new signs of ocular or extraocular issues.
Eyes PERRL confirmed during initial and follow-up exams.
All pre-hospital protocols have been followed regarding stroke management.
Based on current assessment, no new information suggests another condition.
The altered mental status is consistent with a transient issue at this time.
No new neurological findings during transport phase.
The patient remains animated with no new focal motor deficits.
No new signs of dysphasia progress or regression in the field.
No new evidence of nuchal rigidity is detected at the time of reassessment.
No new signs of vertebrobasilar insufficiency or signs of haemorrhage observed.
His semi-Fowler's position is designed to facilitate ease in hospital transition and CT preparation.
There have been no changes in rhythm to suggest arrhythmia is involved.
Follow-up GCS is consistent for A&O.
All incidents are logged per stroke protocol including signs before and during transport.
Reassurance for the family that transport is to a stroke-capable facility.
No complications reported in the pre-hospital phase regarding stroke management.
We believe he is a candidate for acute stroke intervention upon confirmation via CT scan.
Everything performed is per Stroke/TIA (Protocol 3-5).
Time of symptom onset is consistent with acute infarct timeline.
We are maintaining a specialized position for comfort and protocol adherence.
CT scan is required to differentiate between haemorrhage and infarction.
All signs point to stroke assessment for which a CT will be the first step in treatment.
No external signs suggesting complications at the time of arrival to resuscitation.
The patient's body position is set up for rapid movement to CT scanner once at hospital.
Time is brain in this situation - we had appropriate coordination with on-scene and receiving hospital.
I have documented all stroke protocol-related activities for the report.
We will be transporting the patient safely and with extra care due to stroke concerns.
All actions taken are consistent with stroke TSI protocol.
Protocol choice is supported by symptoms and neurological deficits observed.
Patient currently stable, and en route to appropriate destination with ongoing monitoring.
Ongoing goal is to maintain physiological stability during transfer for neurological evaluation.
Concerns center on neurological integrity based on symptoms and stroke pre-hospital evaluation.
Final destination is a stroke center for further CT imaging and possible evaluation.
All expected urgent care links are in place and being followed in real-time.
There are no signs of another emergent condition over time in this case.
We continue to monitor for variations in GCS or neurological status.
GCS score tracked and trauma concerned areas are complete and within protocol.
On our arrival he will be moved quickly for assessment to identify stroke subtype.
All documentation in line with Stroke/TIA protocol is completed and accurate.
His condition as of last check was stable with A&O status and normal functioning.
On our arrival he will be moved quickly for assessment to identify stroke subtype.
All initial stroke findings have been noted and communicated to on-call hospital staff.
Pre-hospital treatments are consistent with stroke-specific best practice.
Next step is CT scan, followed by targeted management at the hospital level.
Patient will be more comfortable maintaining current head position for ease in hospital movement.
All assessments reviewed and unchanged as of latest check.
Patient met all criteria for stroke suspect and transport to Stroke Center.
No difficulty encountered in the performance of IV insertion as per study.
4-lead has been maintained throughout the duration.
No significant changes in rhythm while in transport condition.
No sign of worsening neurological status during our surveillance period.
Stroke management process is clear and factual, tracking real time with GCS and CT imaging.
All initial interventions performed in line with initial field impression and medical staff protocol.
We are transferring to a certified trauma care center to conduct further evaluations.
Patient remains in good shape with ongoing neurological assessments and physical rechecks.
Transport destination established as target hospital with acute stroke capabilities.
We confirmed and provided full stroke pre-hospital information to the on-call neuro hospital staff.
The medical team at receiving hospital has access to complete stroke care.
The patient will proceed with CT scan to evaluate for infarction or haemorrhage.
Stroke and TIA lead-off modules are coherent with patient condition in transport.
Final criteria in the decision tree led to a CT-ordered stroke management approach.
Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time (A&O x3) as per the initial assessment.
